passages.title: Cloze Test 17-21
passages.text: (I) Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice. (II) It is remarkably strong, lightweight, and an excellent conductor of heat and electricity. (III) Scientists believe it could revolutionize industries ranging from electronics to energy storage. (IV) Diamonds are another form of carbon, but their atoms are arranged in a three-dimensional crystal structure. (V) Its potential applications include flexible screens, faster computer chips, and more efficient solar cells.

passages.title: Cloze Test 22-26
passages.text: (1) Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants and some other organisms use sunlight to synthesize foods. (2) This process generally involves the green pigment chlorophyll and generates oxygen as a byproduct. (3) Most plants need water and carbon dioxide to perform photosynthesis effectively. (4) Animals, however, obtain their energy by consuming other organisms. (5) Without photosynthesis, there would be little oxygen in the atmosphere for life to exist.

questions.question: (I) The Silk Road was not a single thoroughfare but a network of interconnected trade routes that spanned from China to the Mediterranean. (II) These routes facilitated the exchange of goods such as silk, spices, and precious metals between East and West. (III) Marco Polo is perhaps the most famous European traveler to have documented his experiences along these paths. (IV) Besides commerce, the Silk Road served as a vital conduit for the spread of ideas, technologies, and religions. (V) Consequently, it played a pivotal role in the development of civilizations across the Eurasian continent.

(I) Epigenetics refers to the study of changes in organisms caused by modification of gene expression rather than alteration of the genetic code itself. (II) These changes can be influenced by various environmental factors, including diet, stress, and exposure to pollutants. (III) Watson and Crick are credited with discovering the double helix structure of DNA in 1953. (IV) Research indicates that epigenetic marks can sometimes be passed down from one generation to the next. (V) This phenomenon suggests that an individual's lifestyle choices could potentially impact the health of their descendants.

(I) The concept of the 'Uncanny Valley' describes the sense of unease felt when human-like robots or animations appear almost, but not quite, human. (II) As the resemblance increases, there is a point where the observer's emotional response turns from empathy to revulsion. (III) Advances in artificial intelligence have made it possible for robots to perform complex surgical procedures with high precision. (IV) This drop in comfort occurs because the subtle imperfections make the entity seem eerie or corpse-like. (V) Designers of digital avatars must navigate this psychological threshold carefully to ensure user acceptance.

(I) In economics, the tragedy of the commons occurs when individuals act independently according to their own self-interest. (II) In doing so, they deplete a shared resource, even if it is clear that it is not in anyone's long-term interest. (III) Common examples include overfishing in international waters and the overgrazing of public lands. (IV) Adam Smith’s 'invisible hand' theory suggests that individual pursuit of profit generally benefits society. (V) To mitigate this issue, governments often implement regulations or privatization strategies.

(I) Dark matter is a hypothetical form of matter that is thought to account for approximately 85% of the matter in the universe. (II) It does not interact with electromagnetic radiation, such as light, making it invisible to conventional telescopes. (III) Astronomers infer its existence from the gravitational effects it exerts on visible matter and the large-scale structure of the universe. (IV) Dark energy, on the other hand, is believed to be responsible for the accelerating expansion of the cosmos. (V) Despite its prevalence, its exact composition remains one of the greatest mysteries in modern physics.

(I) Urbanization is the process through which cities grow and higher percentages of the population come to live in the city. (II) This shift is often driven by the search for better economic opportunities and improved access to services. (III) However, rapid urban growth can lead to significant challenges, such as inadequate housing and increased pollution. (IV) Modern architecture often emphasizes sustainability and the use of eco-friendly building materials. (V) Effective urban planning is therefore essential to manage the complexities of expanding metropolitan areas.

(I) Photosynthesis is a biological process used by plants and other organisms to convert light energy into chemical energy. (II) During this process, carbon dioxide and water are transformed into glucose and oxygen. (III) Chlorophyll, the green pigment in leaves, plays a crucial role by absorbing sunlight. (IV) Global warming is primarily caused by the excessive release of greenhouse gases like carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. (V) The energy stored in glucose molecules then fuels the plant's metabolic activities.

(I) The Renaissance was a period of intense cultural, artistic, and scientific rebirth in Europe. (II) It marked the transition from the Middle Ages to modernity, beginning in Italy in the 14th century. (III) Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo are often cited as the quintessential 'Renaissance Men' due to their diverse talents. (IV) The invention of the steam engine later triggered the Industrial Revolution in the 18th century. (V) This era saw a renewed interest in the classical philosophy and literature of ancient Greece and Rome.

(I) Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon where two or more particles become connected in such a way that the state of one instantly influences the state of the other. (II) This connection persists even when the particles are separated by vast distances. (III) Einstein famously referred to this occurrence as 'spooky action at a distance.' (IV) General relativity describes how gravity works on a macroscopic scale, such as with planets and stars. (V) The implications of entanglement are central to the development of quantum computing and cryptography.

(I) Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is a psycho-social intervention that aims to improve mental health. (II) It focuses on challenging and changing unhelpful cognitive distortions and behaviors. (III) Unlike traditional psychoanalysis, CBT is usually short-term and goal-oriented. (IV) Sigmund Freud is known as the father of psychoanalysis and developed theories on the unconscious mind. (V) Studies have shown that it is effective for a wide range of conditions, including depression and anxiety disorders.

(I) The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, located off the coast of Australia. (II) It supports a diverse array of marine life, including thousands of species of fish and mollusks. (III) In recent years, the reef has faced significant threats from coral bleaching caused by rising ocean temperatures. (IV) Tourism is a major industry in Australia, contributing billions of dollars to the national economy. (V) Conservation efforts are underway to protect this fragile ecosystem from further degradation.

(I) Gothic architecture is a style that flourished in Europe during the High and Late Middle Ages. (II) It is characterized by the use of pointed arches, ribbed vaults, and flying buttresses. (III) These features allowed for the construction of taller buildings with thinner walls and larger windows. (IV) The Palace of Versailles is a classic example of French Baroque architecture. (V) Stained glass windows were also a prominent feature, flooding cathedrals with light and color.

(I) The Pareto Principle, also known as the 80/20 rule, suggests that 80% of effects come from 20% of causes. (II) In a business context, this often means that a small minority of customers generate the majority of revenue. (III) Time management experts often use this principle to help individuals prioritize their most productive tasks. (IV) Economies of scale occur when the cost per unit decreases as the volume of production increases. (V) By focusing on the vital few rather than the trivial many, efficiency can be significantly improved.

(I) Plate tectonics is the scientific theory that describes the large-scale motion of the plates making up Earth's lithosphere. (II) This movement is driven by the heat generated from the planet's interior. (III) At plate boundaries, geological activities such as earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and mountain building occur. (IV) The Richter scale is used to measure the magnitude of the energy released during an earthquake. (V) These plates are constantly moving, albeit very slowly, shifting continents over millions of years.

(I) Linguistic relativity, or the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, suggests that the structure of a language influences its speakers' worldview. (II) According to this theory, people who speak different languages perceive the world in fundamentally different ways. (III) Many linguists argue that while language influences thought, it does not strictly determine it. (IV) Learning a second language has been shown to improve cognitive flexibility and delay the onset of dementia. (V) Despite ongoing debate, the hypothesis remains a central topic in the study of cognitive linguistics.

(I) The Enlightenment was an intellectual and philosophical movement that dominated the world of ideas in Europe during the 18th century. (II) It emphasized reason, logic, and the scientific method as the primary sources of authority. (III) Thinkers of this era, such as Voltaire and Rousseau, advocated for individual liberty and religious tolerance. (IV) The French Revolution, which began in 1789, was partly a reaction against the absolute monarchy. (V) These ideals laid the groundwork for modern democratic societies and the separation of church and state.

(I) The concept of Soft Power, developed by Joseph Nye, refers to the ability of a country to persuade others without the use of force or coercion. (II) It relies heavily on the attractiveness of a nation's culture, political ideals, and policies. (III) Hard power, by contrast, involves military intervention and economic sanctions to achieve diplomatic goals. (IV) Most modern conflicts are characterized by asymmetrical warfare between state and non-state actors. (V) Countries often use public diplomacy and cultural exchange programs to enhance their soft power globally.

(I) Urban heat islands occur when cities replace natural land cover with dense concentrations of pavement and buildings. (II) These surfaces absorb and retain heat, leading to significantly higher temperatures in urban areas compared to rural surroundings. (III) This effect can exacerbate the impact of heatwaves and increase energy consumption for cooling. (IV) Agricultural subsidies are often criticized for distorting international trade markets. (V) Strategies to mitigate this include planting more trees and using reflective roofing materials.

(I) In linguistics, the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis suggests that the structure of a language affects its speakers' world view or cognition. (II) Linguistic relativity is the weaker version of this idea, proposing that language influences rather than determines thought. (III) Many researchers have conducted studies on color perception to test this theory. (IV) Learning a second language in adulthood is generally more difficult than acquiring one during childhood. (V) Although controversial, the hypothesis remains a central topic of debate in cognitive science.

(I) The Great Depression was the worst economic downturn in the history of the industrialized world, lasting from 1929 to 1939. (II) It began after the stock market crash of October 1929, which sent Wall Street into a panic and wiped out millions of investors. (III) During this period, consumer spending and investment dropped, causing steep declines in industrial output. (IV) The Federal Reserve was created in 1913 to provide the nation with a safer and more flexible monetary system. (V) High unemployment rates led to widespread poverty and the emergence of shanty towns known as Hoovervilles.

(I) Neuroplasticity is the brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. (II) This process allows the neurons in the brain to compensate for injury and disease. (III) Mental activities and physical exercise have been shown to promote positive neuroplastic changes. (IV) Parkinson's disease is primarily characterized by the loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the brain. (V) It enables the brain to adjust its activities in response to new situations or changes in the environment.

(I) Architecture in the Renaissance was characterized by a revival of classical Greek and Roman forms. (II) Architects emphasized symmetry, proportion, and geometry as seen in the works of antiquity. (III) The use of columns, domes, and arches became central elements of building design. (IV) Modernist architecture, however, rejected ornamentation in favor of functionalism and minimalism. (V) Filippo Brunelleschi was one of the pioneers who rediscovered the principles of linear perspective during this era.

(I) Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms are increasingly being used to diagnose medical conditions from imaging data. (II) In many cases, these systems can identify patterns that are invisible to the human eye. (III) Medical ethics requires that patients give informed consent before undergoing any procedure. (IV) For instance, AI has shown remarkable accuracy in detecting early-stage skin cancer and retinal diseases. (V) However, the integration of these tools into clinical practice requires rigorous validation to ensure safety.

(I) The Enlightenment was an intellectual movement that dominated the world of ideas in Europe during the 18th century. (II) It was centered on the idea that reason is the primary source of authority and legitimacy. (III) Philosophers of this period advocated for ideals such as liberty, progress, and the separation of church and state. (IV) The French Revolution later sought to implement many of these radical concepts. (V) Steam engines were the primary source of power for factories during the subsequent industrial era.

(I) Marine biology focuses on the study of organisms in the ocean and other saltwater environments. (II) It covers a wide range of topics, from the behavior of individual creatures to the health of entire ecosystems. (III) Ocean acidification, caused by increased CO2 absorption, poses a significant threat to coral reefs. (IV) Freshwater ecosystems like lakes and rivers contain much lower salt concentrations than oceans. (V) Research in this field is vital for understanding the impacts of climate change on biodiversity.

(I) Gothic fiction is a genre of literature that combines elements of both horror and romance. (II) It typically features gloomy settings such as haunted castles, dark forests, and crumbling ruins. (III) Famous examples include Mary Shelley's Frankenstein and Bram Stoker's Dracula. (IV) Science fiction often explores the potential consequences of scientific and technological innovations. (V) The genre is also known for its focus on the sublime and the uncanny to evoke fear in the reader.

(I) Sociology is the study of social life, social change, and the social causes and consequences of human behavior. (II) Sociologists investigate the structure of groups, organizations, and societies. (III) They use various research methods, such as surveys and ethnography, to gather data. (IV) Psychology, on the other hand, focuses more on individual mental processes and behavior. (V) The primary goal is to understand how social forces shape individual lives and collective actions.

(I) Plate tectonics is the scientific theory that describes the large-scale motion of the plates making up Earth's lithosphere. (II) This movement is driven by the heat generated from the radioactive decay of elements in the Earth's interior. (III) The theory explains the formation of mountains, volcanoes, and the occurrence of earthquakes. (IV) Weather patterns are primarily influenced by solar radiation and the rotation of the Earth. (V) It has also helped scientists reconstruct the past positions of continents over millions of years.

(I) Macroeconomics deals with the performance, structure, and behavior of an economy as a whole. (II) It focuses on aggregate changes such as unemployment, growth rate, and gross domestic product. (III) Central banks use monetary policy to influence these variables and stabilize the economy. (IV) Microeconomics examines the decisions made by individual consumers and firms. (V) Models in this field are used to develop government policies and corporate strategies.

(I) The Big Bang theory is the prevailing cosmological model for the observable universe from the earliest known periods. (II) It suggests that the universe began as a very hot, dense point and has been expanding ever since. (III) Astronomers have observed that distant galaxies are moving away from us, supporting this expansion. (IV) Black holes are regions of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing can escape. (V) Cosmic microwave background radiation is considered the afterglow of this initial explosion.

(I) Cybersecurity involves protecting systems, networks, and programs from digital attacks. (II) These attacks are usually aimed at accessing, changing, or destroying sensitive information. (III) Implementing effective measures is particularly challenging today because there are more devices than people. (IV) Many companies are now moving their data to cloud-based storage for better accessibility. (V) Therefore, a successful cybersecurity approach has multiple layers of protection spread across the computers and data.

(I) Sustainable agriculture aims to meet society's present food needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs. (II) It integrates three main goals: environmental health, economic profitability, and social equity. (III) Techniques include crop rotation, integrated pest management, and the use of cover crops. (IV) Genetically modified organisms (GMOs) have been a subject of intense debate regarding food safety. (V) By focusing on soil health, these practices help maintain productivity over the long term.

(I) Cognitive psychology is the scientific study of mental processes such as attention, language use, memory, and perception. (II) It emerged in the 1950s as a reaction to behaviorism, which focused only on observable actions. (III) Researchers in this field use experimental methods to infer internal mental states. (IV) Behavioral therapy is often used to treat phobias by gradually exposing patients to their fears. (V) This discipline has greatly influenced other areas like artificial intelligence and educational theory.
